preFix.title: Oak wall shelf with through-tenon bracket
preFix.subtitle: A simple shelf supported by through-tenon brackets in English oak
preFix.excerpt: A wall shelf in English oak with two bracketed supports joined to the shelf rail by through-tenons. The tenon ends pass completely through the bracket and are locked with a wedge driven into a saw kerf, locking the joint without glue. An entry-level project in seasoned wood that introduces mortise-and-tenon layout and chopping.
preFix.sourceNotes: Newey and Drage Practical Carpentry and Joinery for mortise-and-tenon proportions and layout. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III for through-tenon construction and wedging.

content.text: Cutting tools are sharp. Work with the blade travelling away from your body and your free hand behind the cutting edge at all times. Strop the tool before starting and whenever the edge feels rough.
This shelf uses two L-shaped bracket supports, each pierced by a
through-tenon

from the shelf rail. The tenon end protrudes on the outer face of the bracket and is locked with a wedge. No screws or glue are needed; the wedge tightens the joint mechanically. The finished shelf is roughly 60 cm wide and 18 cm deep, with bracket arms 20 cm high.
Materials
A shelf board of
seasoned

English oak, 60 cm x 18 cm x 18 mm. Two bracket pieces, each 20 cm x 10 cm x 40 mm. Two wedge blanks cut from offcuts, each 60 mm long and tapered from 8 mm to 2 mm across their width. Three wall fixings to mount the back of the shelf board to the wall.
Laying out the mortise

Set the marking gauge to one-third the bracket thickness (about 13 mm for a 40 mm bracket). Scribe the mortise width on both faces of the bracket. Mark the mortise length at 30 mm (matching the shelf board depth). Score the mortise ends with the marking knife and try-square to give a clean shoulder line.
Chopping the mortise

Clamp the bracket firmly to the bench with the g-cramp. Using the mortise chisel, chop in from one face to roughly half depth, then flip the bracket and chop through from the other face. Work in 3 mm increments, levering out the waste between chops. Check that the
mortise
walls are clean and square before cutting the tenon.
Cutting the tenon

Mark the tenon on the end of the shelf rail to match the mortise dimensions. Saw the cheeks with the tenon saw, keeping the blade on the waste side of the line. Saw the shoulders across the grain. Test the fit: the tenon should slide through the mortise with hand pressure and no play. Adjust with the chisel if it is tight.
Wedging the tenon
With the tenon pushed fully through the bracket, saw a
kerf

down the centre of the protruding tenon end, about 20 mm deep. Drive the wedge into the kerf with a mallet until the joint is tight. The wedge spreads the tenon inside the mortise and locks it mechanically. Trim the protruding tenon and wedge flush with the outer face of the bracket.
Finishing

Finish with two coats of danish oil applied with a cloth, allowing the first coat to absorb fully before the second. Fix the brackets to the wall studs and lay the shelf board on top, securing through the back edge.
Variations
Pegged tenon: instead of a wedge, drill a peg hole through the bracket and tenon after assembly and drive in a round oak peg. Stronger in shear, and removes the protruding wedge end.
Longer shelf: for a shelf over 80 cm, add a third central bracket. The tenon spacing should be no more than 35 cm.

rows.fix: Sight along the chisel handle at the start of each chop to check it is square to the face. Work in small increments rather than one deep stroke.
rows.cause: Chisel not held perpendicular to the face during chopping
rows.symptom: Mortise walls are ragged and not vertical

rows.fix: A loose tenon can be shimmed with a thin wedge of veneer glued to one cheek. For future joints, saw on the waste side of the knife line, not through the line.
rows.cause: Tenon cheeks cut outside the gauge lines
rows.symptom: Tenon fits loosely in the mortise with visible gaps

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
